questions.stem: In an agents interview, what distinguishes an AI agent from a single LLM completion?
options: Agents plan/act via tools and feedback loops toward a goal, not one-shot text
Agents cannot call tools and may only emit a final paragraph once
Any chatbot with a system prompt is legally defined as an autonomous agent
Agents replace LLMs entirely with finite-state machines and no models
questions.explanation: Agency implies iterative perception–reasoning–action with external effects.

questions.stem: ReAct prompting pattern: what two interleaved activities does the pattern emphasize?
options: Only final answers without revealing intermediate reasoning or actions
Reasoning traces (thoughts) alternating with concrete tool/environment actions
Training a reward model online during every user session automatically
Replacing tools with larger context windows until actions are unnecessary
questions.explanation: ReAct couples chain-of-thought with observable tool use steps.

questions.stem: Tool use / function calling: what must the runtime provide beyond the LLM itself?
options: Only a larger temperature so the model invents tool outputs itself
A guarantee that all tool calls succeed without error handling
Schemas, execution sandbox, auth, and a way to return observations to the model
Removal of the system prompt so tools become the only instructions
questions.explanation: Orchestration executes tools safely and feeds results back into context.

questions.stem: Short-term versus long-term agent memory: how do you distinguish them clearly?
options: Long-term memory is only the GPU VRAM holding model weights
Short-term memory forbids tool calls; long-term forbids prompts
They are identical Redis keys with different marketing names
Short-term is session/context state; long-term persists knowledge across sessions
questions.explanation: Persistence horizon separates scratchpad context from durable stores.

questions.stem: Multi-agent systems: what potential benefit should you mention carefully in interviews?
options: Specialization and critique among roles can improve complex workflows
More agents always reduce cost and latency compared with one agent
Multi-agent setups eliminate the need for any evaluation or guardrails
Agents cannot communicate; multi-agent only means sharding GPUs
questions.explanation: Role-split agents help when tasks decompose, but add coordination cost.

questions.stem: LangGraph-style idea: what does a graph-based agent orchestration buy your system?
options: Automatic training of new foundation models from the graph structure
Explicit nodes/edges for control flow, retries, branching, and stateful loops
Deletion of tools because graphs replace all external side effects
A guarantee of optimal plans without any search or evaluation
questions.explanation: Graphs make agent control flow inspectable and engineered, not ad hoc.

questions.stem: Guardrails for agents: which controls belong on a production readiness checklist?
options: Removing all authentication so tools are easier to call quickly
Letting the agent self-modify its own production credentials freely
Allowlists, spend limits, sandboxes, output filters, and policy checks
Guardrails only apply to training and never to runtime tool calls
questions.explanation: Defense in depth constrains what agents may say and do.

questions.stem: Agent loops that never terminate: what mitigations should you design into the system?
options: Increase temperature until the loop becomes creative enough to stop
Disable logging so infinite loops consume resources silently
Infinite loops are impossible if the model uses ReAct formatting
Max steps, timeouts, budget caps, and stop conditions on goal checks
questions.explanation: Hard resource limits prevent runaway tool-calling cycles.

questions.stem: Human-in-the-loop (HITL): when is it especially important for production agents?
options: High-stakes, irreversible, or ambiguous actions needing approval gates
Only for unit tests and never for production user workflows
HITL means the human types tokens instead of using an LLM at all
Agents with tools never need humans because tools are sandboxed always
questions.explanation: Approval checkpoints reduce blast radius on consequential operations.

questions.stem: Agentic RAG: how does it differ from classic retrieve-then-read RAG pipelines?
options: Agentic RAG forbids vector indexes and uses only parametric memory
The agent decides when/what to retrieve iteratively based on intermediate needs
Classic RAG already plans arbitrary tool sequences by definition
Agentic means the retriever trains the generator online each query
questions.explanation: Retrieval becomes a controllable action inside a broader agent policy.

questions.stem: Planning then execution vs interleaved ReAct: what tradeoff appears in practice?
options: Planning always dominates because environments never change mid-task
Interleaved methods cannot use tools and only produce text plans
Up-front plans can be brittle; interleaved act/observe adapts to feedback
They are identical if the context window exceeds one thousand tokens
questions.explanation: Closed-loop execution handles uncertainty better than open-loop plans.

questions.stem: Observation quality: why do bad tool outputs break otherwise capable agents?
options: Agents ignore tool results by architecture and only read the user prompt
Tool outputs are encrypted so models cannot misuse them anyway
Observation noise always improves exploration without downside
Garbage observations poison the next reasoning step and compound errors
questions.explanation: Agents are only as good as the feedback signals tools return.

questions.stem: Stateful tools (browsers, REPLs): what extra risks should you call out in interviews?
options: Session drift, side effects, credential exposure, and non-idempotent actions
Stateful tools cannot affect the world beyond returning strings
Browsers used by agents are read-only by networking protocol law
REPLs delete the need for sandboxing because code is already text
questions.explanation: Persistent environments amplify both capability and security risk.

questions.stem: Evaluation of agents: why are single-turn LLM benchmarks insufficient alone?
options: Agent quality equals MMLU score of the underlying base model always
Agents fail via multi-step trajectories, tool misuse, and loop pathologies
Only latency matters; task success rates are irrelevant for agents
Trajectory evals are impossible because tools are nondeterministic forever
questions.explanation: You must score end-to-end task success and intermediate step quality.

questions.stem: Memory write policies: what should agents store versus leave as ephemeral context?
options: Store every token of every tool response forever without summarization
Never write memory because context windows make persistence obsolete
Durable facts/preferences worth reuse; avoid secrets and stale noisy traces
Memory stores must include raw API keys for faster tool authentication
questions.explanation: Selective memory prevents bloat, leakage, and incorrect future grounding.

questions.stem: Supervisor / worker multi-agent pattern: what division of labor is typical here?
options: Every worker independently ships code to production without review
Supervisors only resize GPU clusters and never see task content
Workers must all use identical prompts with zero specialization
A coordinator delegates subtasks to specialist workers and synthesizes results
questions.explanation: Hierarchical control helps decompose work while keeping a single owner.

questions.stem: Deterministic workflows vs free-form agents: when should you prefer explicit graphs?
options: When compliance, SLAs, and predictable steps matter more than open exploration
Always prefer free-form agents because graphs cannot call tools
Deterministic workflows cannot include an LLM at any node
Graphs are only for training and free-form is only for evaluation
questions.explanation: Engineered control flow wins when variance and auditability dominate.

questions.stem: Tool permission scopes: what least-privilege example belongs in agent interviews?
options: Grant admin cloud credentials to every agent for maximum helpfulness
Read-only search tools by default; escalate writes behind HITL approval
Put secrets in prompts so tools can parse them without a vault
Scopes are unnecessary if the model says it will be careful
questions.explanation: Narrow capabilities reduce damage from prompt injection and bugs.

questions.stem: Reflection / self-critique steps: what are they intended to improve in agents?
options: Increase token usage with no intended quality benefit by design
Replace external evaluators so metrics become unnecessary forever
Catch errors, missing evidence, or policy violations before finalizing actions
Reflection deletes tool APIs and answers from parametric memory only
questions.explanation: Critique loops can reduce avoidable mistakes in long trajectories.

questions.stem: Cost control for agent systems: which levers should you mention first in design?
options: Always use the largest model for every micro-decision without routing
Disable caching because repeated tool calls improve freshness always
Cost is fixed per user and cannot be influenced by agent design
Step caps, cheaper models for routing, caching, and selective tool use
questions.explanation: Token and tool budgets are first-class product constraints for agents.

questions.stem: Blackboard vs message-passing multi-agent designs: what contrast is useful to know?
options: Blackboard shares a common state; message-passing exchanges explicit messages
Blackboard forbids any shared state by networking definition
Message-passing requires a single agent and cannot scale out
Both designs prohibit tools and only allow pure text chat
questions.explanation: Coordination style affects observability, contention, and coupling.

questions.stem: Formal planning (PDDL-like) plus LLMs: when might classical planners help agents?
options: Classical planners replace all perception and need no world model
When actions have clear preconditions/effects and need reliable long plans
LLMs cannot interface with planners because plans are not text
PDDL is required for every web browsing agent by law
questions.explanation: Symbolic planners excel at structured action spaces with constraints.

questions.stem: Environment grounding: why do agents need reliable schemas and validators on tools?
options: Schemas slow agents down and should be avoided in production
Validators are only for SQL databases and not HTTP tools
Malformed tool calls waste steps; validators catch errors early
LLMs always emit perfect JSON if temperature is below 0.7
questions.explanation: Structured interfaces turn free-form text into executable actions safely.

questions.stem: Debate / critique multi-agent setups: what failure mode still remains a risk?
options: Debate always converges to ground truth if agents use different temperatures
Critique agents cannot hallucinate because they only read others’ text
Multi-agent debate removes the need for external tools and retrieval
Shared blind spots and persuasive wrong arguments can reinforce errors
questions.explanation: Social aggregation helps but does not guarantee correctness.

questions.stem: Long-running agents: what operational concerns dominate beyond raw model quality?
options: Checkpointing, idempotency, retries, observability, and recovery from crashes
Only the system prompt matters; ops tooling is optional for uptime
Long-running agents cannot be observed because trajectories are private
Idempotency is automatic for all HTTP POST tool calls
questions.explanation: Production agents are distributed systems problems, not just prompts.

questions.stem: Policy engines alongside LLMs: what separation of duties is healthy for agents?
options: Put all policies only in natural language prompts with no code checks
Hard rules enforce non-negotiable constraints; LLMs handle flexible reasoning
Policy engines should be editable by the agent at runtime freely
LLMs must bypass policies when users say the request is urgent
questions.explanation: Code-enforced policies catch violations models might rationalize away.

questions.stem: Memory retrieval for agents: how is it similar to and different from classic RAG?
options: Agent memory cannot use embeddings and must store full transcripts only
RAG indexes are writeable by end users mid-conversation always
Similar vector/search tech, but memories are personalized, temporal, and writeable
Memory retrieval forbids citations and grounding checks by design
questions.explanation: Personal memory needs lifecycle, privacy, and conflict resolution extras.

questions.stem: Tool hallucination: what does it mean and how do you mitigate it in agents?
options: Tool hallucination means the sandbox ran out of CPU quota
It only occurs when using sparse retrieval without dense vectors
Allowing free-form eval() of model text is the recommended mitigation
Model invents APIs/args; mitigate with strict schemas and allowlisted tools
questions.explanation: Never execute free-form invented calls; bind to declared tool contracts.

questions.stem: Parallel tool calls: when are they beneficial inside a single agent reasoning step?
options: Independent I/O-bound lookups can run concurrently to cut wall-clock time
Parallelism always helps even when tools have write conflicts
Agents cannot call more than one tool per session by protocol
Parallel calls remove the need for any error handling on failures
questions.explanation: Safe concurrency requires independence and conflict-aware design.

questions.stem: Simulation environments for agent eval: why invest in them early in the project?
options: Simulators always match production perfectly with zero domain gap
Cheap, repeatable stress tests of plans/tools without harming production
Real user traffic is the only ethical way to test irreversible actions
Simulations cannot measure loop length or tool misuse rates
questions.explanation: Sandboxed worlds catch failure modes before they hit real systems.

questions.stem: Escalation policies: what should trigger handoff from an agent to a human operator?
options: Every greeting message must escalate to a human for compliance
Agents should never escalate because that admits model weakness
Low confidence, policy ambiguity, repeated failures, or high-impact actions
Escalation is only for billing questions and never for safety issues
questions.explanation: Clear escalation beats silent wrong action in uncertain states.

questions.stem: Graph cycles in agent workflows: when are loops desirable versus dangerous?
options: Cycles are always bugs and must be forbidden in all agent graphs
Loops guarantee optimality if the model uses chain-of-thought
Budgets are unnecessary when cycles include a reflection node
Desirable for refine/retry; dangerous without budgets and convergence checks
questions.explanation: Controlled iteration helps; unbounded cycles burn money and time.

questions.stem: Shared tool registries in multi-agent systems: what governance is needed day-to-day?
options: Versioned schemas, ownership, rate limits, and change management
Any agent may edit production tool code live without review
Registries should hide schemas so models invent arguments freely
Governance only matters for single-agent systems, not multi-agent
questions.explanation: Tools are APIs—treat them with platform engineering discipline.

questions.stem: Offline RL / trajectory optimization for agents: what data do you typically need?
options: Only unlabeled prompts without any notion of task success
Logged state-action-observation outcomes with reward/success labels
Trajectories cannot be used because agents are non-stationary forever
Offline methods require live production writes on every gradient step
questions.explanation: Learning from logs needs outcomes, not just text demos.

questions.stem: Prompt injection against agents with tools: why is the blast radius often larger?
options: Injection only changes font size in the UI without tool impact
Tool-using agents are immune because tools validate all natural language
Injected instructions can trigger real side-effecting tool invocations
Blast radius shrinks automatically when adding more tools
questions.explanation: Untrusted content plus privileged tools is a high-severity combo.

questions.stem: Capability routing: small model vs large model inside an agent orchestration graph?
options: Always use the largest model for string equality checks too
Small models cannot participate in graphs that also contain large models
Routing is only about GPUs, not about task difficulty or cost
Route easy classification/extraction to small models; hard reasoning to large
questions.explanation: Heterogeneous model choice optimizes cost/quality per node.

questions.stem: Deterministic replay of agent runs: what do you need to store for effective debugging?
options: Prompts, tool I/O, model versions, seeds/configs, and policy decisions
Only the final answer string without intermediate observations
Replay is impossible because LLMs forbid logging by license
Storing tool outputs violates the definition of an agent trajectory
questions.explanation: Debuggability requires a full audit trail of the control loop.

questions.stem: Contract testing for tools: why do agent platforms invest in schema contracts?
options: Contract tests replace the need for any runtime validation forever
Schema drift breaks agents silently; tests catch incompatible changes early
Tools never change once published so contracts are optional
Agents invent new fields so contracts should be ignored in prod
questions.explanation: Stable tool interfaces keep agent prompts and parsers working.

questions.stem: Swarm-style many agents: what coordination tax should you anticipate in design?
options: More agents linearly decrease coordination cost forever
Swarms eliminate the need for a goal specification entirely
Communication overhead, conflicting actions, and harder debugging/evals
Conflicts cannot occur if each agent has a unique temperature
questions.explanation: Parallelism helps only with strong orchestration and conflict rules.

questions.stem: Safety layers order: where should non-bypassable checks live in an agent stack?
options: Only inside the prompt where the model can choose to ignore them
Safety checks should run solely after irreversible side effects complete
The UI layer is sufficient even if backends execute raw model text
In the tool/runtime boundary after the model proposes an action
questions.explanation: Enforce critical policies in code paths the model cannot skip.

questions.stem: Senior agents: how do you design an architecture for bank-transfer-level actions?
options: Typed intents, dual control HITL, strict allowlists, audits, and simulators first
Let the agent call wire APIs directly from free-form natural language
Rely on RLHF alone so the model refuses all harmful transfers forever
Disable logging to protect customer privacy during money movement
questions.explanation: High-stakes domains demand defense-in-depth beyond clever prompting.

questions.stem: When should you avoid multi-agent designs despite their popularity in demos?
options: Always use multi-agent because single agents cannot call more than one tool
Simple tasks where one tool-calling agent is clearer, cheaper, and enough
Never use multi-agent even for specialized parallel research workflows
Multi-agent is required whenever the context window exceeds 8k tokens
questions.explanation: Complexity must pay rent; otherwise prefer a simpler control loop.

questions.stem: How do you measure whether memory writes actually improve agent success rates?
options: Assume more stored tokens always improve outcomes without measurement
Only measure embedding cosine similarity and ignore task completion
A/B with memory on/off, track task success, regressions, and privacy incidents
Memory quality cannot be measured because sessions are unique forever
questions.explanation: Treat memory like a product feature with causal impact evaluation.

questions.stem: LangGraph-like durability (checkpointing): what user-visible failures does it fix?
options: It increases model accuracy on MMLU without changing orchestration
Checkpointing replaces the need for idempotent tool design entirely
Durability means deleting traces so runs cannot be audited later
Resume after crashes mid-workflow without losing completed side-effect state carefully
questions.explanation: Persisted graph state supports reliable long-running business processes.

questions.stem: Combining guardrails, HITL, and sandboxing: what layering principle should guide you?
options: Prevent → detect → contain → recover, with humans on the highest-impact path
Pick exactly one control and delete the others to reduce latency
Sandboxing makes HITL unnecessary for irreversible external actions
Guardrails should run only in staging and be disabled in production
questions.explanation: Overlapping controls assume any single layer can fail.

questions.stem: Agent eval harness design: what scenario types must you include at minimum?
options: Only sunny-day scripts where every tool returns the ideal payload
Happy paths, tool failures, injections, long loops, and ambiguous goals
Exclusive focus on token throughput without task success labels
Evals that forbid simulating errors because errors never happen live
questions.explanation: Adversarial and failure-mode coverage predicts production pain.

questions.stem: Open-ended web agents: what makes them uniquely hard to secure and evaluate well?
options: The web is a closed schema with stable APIs and no injection surface
Browsing agents cannot encounter prompt injection by HTTP design
Huge action space, untrusted content, and moving targets across sites
Evaluation is trivial because success equals number of pages visited
questions.explanation: Open worlds maximize capability and risk simultaneously.

questions.stem: How should product specs define stop conditions for autonomous agent workflows?
options: Stop only when the process is killed by the operating system OOM
Never stop because autonomy means running forever by definition
Stop conditions belong only in marketing docs, not runtime config
Explicit success criteria, refusal rules, budgets, and escalation triggers
questions.explanation: Clear termination criteria turn demos into operable systems.

questions.stem: Team topology for agent platforms: which skills must sit next to prompt authors?
options: Security, SRE/observability, eval engineering, and API/tool owners
Only prompt writers; infrastructure and security can be afterthoughts
Agents remove the need for backend engineers once tools exist
Eval engineering is optional if the demo impresses executives
questions.explanation: Agent products are socio-technical systems needing cross-functional ownership.

questions.stem: Future-proofing agent designs: what abstraction stays valuable as models improve?
options: Hard-coding chain-of-thought text that must never change with new models
Typed tools, policies, state machines/graphs, and measurable eval suites
Assuming one prompt file replaces all software architecture forever
Removing guardrails as soon as benchmark scores cross a threshold
questions.explanation: Control, interfaces, and evals remain even when raw model skill rises.
